From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Financier](/w/index.php?title=Financier&redirect=no "Financier"))

Person who allocates capital with the expectation of a financial return

For the Swedish investment company, see [Investor AB](/wiki/Investor_AB "Investor AB").

An **investor** is a person or [entity](/wiki/Institutional_investor "Institutional investor") that allocates [financial capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital") with the expectation of a future [return](/wiki/Return_on_capital "Return on capital") (profit) or to gain an advantage (interest).[[1]](#cite_note-Reasonable-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Through this allocated capital the investor usually purchases some species of property.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Types of [investments](/wiki/Investment "Investment") include [equity](/wiki/Stock "Stock"), [debt](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)"), [securities](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)"), [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure"), [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency"), [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity"), [token](/wiki/Exonumia "Exonumia"), derivatives such as put and call [options](/wiki/Option_(finance) "Option (finance)"), [futures](/wiki/Futures_contract "Futures contract"), [forwards](/wiki/Forward_contract "Forward contract"), etc. This definition makes no distinction between the investors in the [primary](/wiki/Primary_market "Primary market") and [secondary markets](/wiki/Secondary_market "Secondary market"). That is, someone who provides a business with capital and someone who buys a stock are both investors. An investor who owns stock is a [shareholder](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder").

## Types of investors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Investor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types of investors")]

There are two types of investors: [retail investors](/wiki/Retail_investor "Retail investor") and [institutional investors](/wiki/Institutional_investor "Institutional investor").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

A *retail investor* is also known as an *individual investor*.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

There are several sub-types of institutional investor:

* [Pension plans](/wiki/Pension_plan "Pension plan") making investments on behalf of employees
* [Businesses](/wiki/Business "Business") that make investments, either directly or via a captive fund
* [Endowment funds](/wiki/Endowment_fund "Endowment fund") used by universities, churches, etc.
* [Mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund"), [hedge funds](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund"), and other funds, ownership of which may or may not be [publicly traded](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") (these funds typically pool money raised from their owner-subscribers to invest in securities)
* [Sovereign wealth funds](/wiki/Sovereign_wealth_fund "Sovereign wealth fund")
* Large money managers[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Investors might also be classified according to their [profiles](/wiki/Investor_profile "Investor profile"). In this respect, an important distinctive [investor psychology](/wiki/Behavioral_economics "Behavioral economics") trait is [risk attitude](/wiki/Risk_aversion "Risk aversion").

## Investor protection through government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Investor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Investor protection through government")]

Investor protection through government involves regulations and enforcement by government agencies to ensure that market is fair and fraudulent activities are eliminated. An example of a government agency that protects investors is the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC), which works to protect reasonable investors in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[1]](#cite_note-Reasonable-1)

Similar protections exist in other countries, including the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") where individual investors have certain protections via the [Financial Services Compensation Scheme](/wiki/Financial_Services_Compensation_Scheme "Financial Services Compensation Scheme") (FSCS).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Investment tax structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Investor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Investment tax structures")]

Company [dividends](/wiki/Dividend "Dividend") are paid from [net income](/wiki/Net_income "Net income"), which has the tax already deducted. Therefore, shareholders are given some respite with a preferential tax rate of 15% on "[qualified dividends](/wiki/Qualified_dividend "Qualified dividend")" in the event of the company being domiciled in the United States. Alternatively, in another country having a [double-taxation](/wiki/Double-taxation "Double-taxation") treaty with the US, accepted by the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") (IRS). Non-qualified dividends paid by other foreign companies or entities; for example, those receiving income derived from interest on bonds held by a mutual fund, are taxed at the regular and generally higher rate of income tax. When applied to 2013, this is on a sliding scale up to 39.6%, with an additional 3.8% surtax for high-income taxpayers ($200,000 for singles, $250,000 for married couples).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Role of the financier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Investor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Role of the financier")]

"Financier" redirects here. For other uses, see [Financier (disambiguation)](/wiki/Financier_(disambiguation) "Financier (disambiguation)").

A **financier** ([/fɪnənˈsɪər, fə-, -ˈnæn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) is a person whose primary occupation is either facilitating or directly providing investments to up-and-coming or established [companies](/wiki/Companies "Companies") and [businesses](/wiki/Businesses "Businesses"), typically involving large sums of money and usually involving [private equity](/wiki/Private_equity "Private equity") and [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital"), [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions"), [leveraged buyouts](/wiki/Leveraged_buyout "Leveraged buyout"), [corporate finance](/wiki/Corporate_finance "Corporate finance"), [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking"), or large-scale [asset management](/wiki/Asset_management "Asset management"). A financier makes money through this process when their investment is paid back with interest,[[11]](#cite_note-Freixas-11) from part of the company's equity awarded to them as specified by the business deal, or a financier can generate income through [commission](/wiki/Commission_(remuneration) "Commission (remuneration)"), performance, and management fees. A financier can also promote the success of a financed business by allowing the business to take advantage of the financier's reputation.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The more experienced and capable the financier is, the more the financier will be able to contribute to the success of the financed entity, and the greater reward the financier will reap.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The term, financier, is [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and derives from *[finance](/wiki/Finance "Finance")* or *payment*.

Financier is someone who handles money. Certain financier avenues require degrees and licenses including [venture capitalists](/wiki/Venture_capitalist "Venture capitalist"), hedge fund managers, trust fund managers, [accountants](/wiki/Accountant "Accountant"), [stockbrokers](/wiki/Stockbroker "Stockbroker"), [financial advisors](/wiki/Financial_advisors "Financial advisors"), or even public [treasurers](/wiki/Treasurer "Treasurer"). Personal investing on the other hand, has no requirements and is open to all using the [stock market](/wiki/Stock_market "Stock market") or by word-of-mouth requests for money. A financier "will be a specialized financial intermediary in the sense that it has experience in [liquidating](/wiki/Liquidation "Liquidation") the type of firm it is lending to".[[11]](#cite_note-Freixas-11)

### Perceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Investor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Perceptions")]

Economist [Edmund Phelps](/wiki/Edmund_Phelps "Edmund Phelps") has argued that the financier plays a role in directing capital to investments that governments and social organizations are constrained from playing:

> [T]he pluralism of experience that the financiers bring to bear in their decisions gives a wide range of entrepreneurial ideas a chance for insightful evaluation. And, importantly, the financier and the entrepreneur do not need the state's or social partners' approval. Nor are they accountable later on to such social bodies if the project goes badly, not even to the financier's investors. So projects that would be too opaque and uncertain for the state or social partners to endorse can be undertaken.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The concept of the financier has been distinguished from that of a mere capitalist based on the asserted higher level of judgment required of the financier.[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, financiers have also been mocked for their perceived tendency to generate wealth at the expense of others, and without engaging in tangible labor. For example, humorist [George Helgesen Fitch](/wiki/George_Helgesen_Fitch "George Helgesen Fitch") described the financier as "a man who can make two dollars grow for himself where one grew for someone else before".[[16]](#cite_note-16)
